data.projectDetailsTitle: Repayment Assistance Plan – Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM)
data.projectDetailsProgram: Canada Student Financial Assistance Program and Canada Apprentice Loan
data.projectDetailsDescription:
In November 2023, the Canada Student Financial Assistance (CSFA) Program introduced a change to the application verification system that supports the Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP) called the Repayment Assistance Plan - Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM). 

RAP-EVM is designed to verify the information declared by borrowers, which are all factors in determining RAP eligibility and calculating RAP payment requirements, during the RAP application process (i.e. income, family size and marital status) against taxpayer information provided by the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA). RAP-EVM applies pre-established business rules to identify applications that meet the selection criteria. Selection criteria were determined using a risk-based sampling methodology to identify high-risk groups more likely to misreport information. The creation of risk group categories was undertaken through statistical analysis of previous cases, in collaboration with ESDC’s Integrity Services Branch. Based on this analysis, CSFA Program identified borrowers with inconsistent family size changes (e.g., if a borrower were to change their response between RAP applications to from a family size of one to a family size above one, and then revert to family size of one), borrowers who claim zero income, and borrowers who report their income just under the RAP zero payment threshold. 

Once selected for verification, a request for taxpayer information (i.e. income, marital status, number of dependants) on these applicants is made to the CRA. If the application data matches CRA taxpayer data, the application is adjudicated for an approval or refusal for RAP. If there are discrepancies, an agent with ESDC’s third-party Service Provider (SP), which operates under banner of the National Student Loans Service Centre (NSLSC), will undertake further verification directly with the borrower. The borrower will then be required to submit additional proof substantiating their information, which an agent will review before making a decision for approval or refusal of the application. 

RAP-EVM replaces the previous, non-authoritative source verification system, which required selected applicants to provide documents to prove that their reported income was accurate; RAP-EVM only requires a borrower to submit additional proof when a discrepancy is identified following the exchange with CRA.

The exchange of borrower and taxpayer information between the SP and the CRA is governed by a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) which outlines the agreement on the information to be exchanged between ESDC and the CRA as well as the conditions and procedures that will apply to the exchange of information. RAP-EVM’s data exchange process between the SP and the CRA is handled via a secure file transfer, known as the Managed Secure File Transfer. Following the exchange of data between CRA and the SP, the RAP application adjudication takes place in a closed environment. Personal information collected and used for the purposes of RAP-EVM is safeguarded under ESDC’s and the CRA’s legal authorities and related policies and directives for collecting and using personal information.    

There are two circumstances under which a borrower may wish to have their RAP decision re-evaluated. Reconsideration is for borrowers that have been refused but can show that unforeseen and unavoidable circumstances have caused them or their spouse or common-law partner to incur exceptional expenses that prevent them from fulfilling their repayment obligations. The other recourse is re-adjudication, which occurs when a borrower has been approved for RAP with a monthly Affordable Payment (AP) but indicates that their gross monthly income has significantly dropped and that they are no longer able to make their AP. 

The Program has completed a Privacy Impact Assessment which was sent to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) in November 2023. Upon review, the OPC recommended that the CFSA Program conduct an Algorithmic Impact Assessment, as the creation of risk group categories in an automated verification process falls within the scope of the Directive on Automated Decision-Making.
data.businessDrivers3: RAP-EVM was implemented to increase the integrity of the CSFA Program RAP benefit following the Office of the Auditor General’s 2020 audit report, which recommended that ESDC take appropriate action to ensure a systemic verification of RAP applications to maximize student loans repayments. However, with the implementation of this new system, clients that are selected for verification could potentially experience faster processing times due to RAP-EVM optimizing the application verification process.
data.businessDrivers4: In 2022-2023, approximately 283,000 borrowers benefited from RAP, and of those, 90% of applicants were approved for a $0 affordable payment. Given the extent of borrowers that benefit from RAP, the introduction of RAP-EVM is anticipated to help ensure program integrity and sound stewardship of public funds. ESDC will make sure that only eligible applicants receive RAP benefits, reducing the potential for misrepresentation and misuse of taxpayer funds.
data.businessDrivers6:
RAP-EVM could result in the following outcomes:
- The new partially automated RAP-EVM system allows the program and service provider to easily identify RAP trends, which ultimately helps to inform evidence-based policy changes.
- Reduced instances of ineligible applicants receiving RAP benefits
- Reduced frequency of misrepresentation
data.businessDrivers7:
Each of the CSFA Program’s systems is governed by guidelines with respect to whom is allowed to access personal information. Specifically, each system has clearly defined user roles to control access to the database, including the use of unique user identifiers and permission settings to view, modify and delete information contained in the database.

Access to taxpayer information provided under the MOU will be controlled and limited to ESDC and SP personnel who:
• Occupy a position that requires access to this information;
• Have a valid Reliability Security Screening Clearance; and
• Have been briefed on the provisions of section 241 and section 239 of the Income Tax Act to maintain the confidentiality of taxpayer information, as well as security measures to safeguard this information as required.

Authorized personnel with Reliability Security Screening Clearance include the following positions: RAP back-office agents, managers and data analysts at the SP, CSFA Program data analysts, and CSFA Program staff working on RAP. 

Borrowers’ personal data are not allowed to be stored in the local hard drives of PCs, laptops and portable devices.
data.businessDrivers8: RAP-EVM may increase timeliness by eliminating the need for a client to provide additional documentation when selected for verification, if no discrepancies between reported information and CRA information are found.
data.businessDrivers10: RAP-EVM replaces the previous, non-authoritative source verification system, which required selected applicants to provide documents to prove that their reported income was accurate; RAP-EVM only requires a borrower to submit additional proof when a discrepancy is identified following the exchange with CRA. Automation using RAP-EVM was the preferred option to decrease the administrative burden on applicants who have no discrepancies between their reported income and proof of income provided by CRA.
data.businessDrivers12: The Office of the Auditor General (OAG) 2020 Spring Report called on ESDC to “ensure proper, systematic verification of RAP applications in order to maximize the repayment of student loan.” Failure to deploy the RAP-EVM system would limit the program’s long-term sustainability.
data.impact31: When an electronic or paper application is received at the SP, the system adjudicates the information using business rules to determine eligibility for RAP. A second set of business rules is triggered when a RAP application is approved to determine whether income, number of dependants, and marital status verification is required using the RAP-EVM. Once data submitted by the borrower has been compared to CRA data, the system then determines if proof from the borrower is required to confirm the information submitted. Throughout these processes, the system uses business rules in the decision-making processes.
data.impact20: There is a RAP formula in the Canada Student Financial Assistance Regulations (CSFAR), which includes family income, family size, and loan balance. The CSFA Program has developed effective methods to identify high-risk groups that are more prone to misreporting information. The program engages RAP-EVM to verify the data of these high-risk groups, as well as of individuals selected for data verification through random sampling.
data.impact21: For RAP-EVM, if the borrower income differs from that at CRA, greater than the allowable variance, a letter is generated to the borrower requesting their proof of income. The same applies to marital status and number of dependants. If the information at CRA differs, proof must be provided by the borrower. In these automated processes, there is no interpretation required.
data.impact8: Impacts from the decision are time limited since RAP approval periods last for six months at a time. During this period, an applicant can always request a re-adjudication due to changes in their income situation, in which case an agent can make changes to the initial RAP calculation decision, provided all the necessary documentation is received. In the event that an applicant receives a RAP refusal, the impact of a refusal is also most likely to be time limited, as clients whose applications are refused may re-apply when/if they meet the requirements as outlined by the CSFAR. An applicant may also appeal a decision on RAP eligibility by submitting exceptional expenses.
data.impact10:
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). 

Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
data.impact25:
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). 

Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
data.impact12:
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). 

Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
data.impact14:
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). 

Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
data.impact16: The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). The only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program by ensuring that only borrowers who meet the eligibility criteria described in the CSFAR receive RAP benefits.
data.aboutDataSource12: Standard ESDC PPU 030 (Student Financial Assistance), Standard ESDC PPU 709 (Canada Apprentice Loans)
data.aboutDataSource13: The input data includes personal information, gross family income, marital status, and number of dependants provided by RAP applicants during the application process. Borrowers can apply for RAP either online or by submitting paper forms. During the exchange, CRA returns a file containing SIN, date of birth, last name, first name, taxation years, income, marital status, and number of dependents.
data.privacyImplementation5:
Privacy Impact Assessment Report 

Canada Student Financial Assistance Program Repayment Assistance Plan – Enhanced Verification Model (CSFA RAP - EVM) 

Signed by Director General of the Canada Student Financial Assistance (CSFA) Program on November 11, 2023
data.decisionSector3:
The following outlines the process flow of RAP-EVM:
1.	Application: The borrower submits their RAP application to the SP, indicating their previous month or current month’s income, married/common-law status and number of dependants. 
a.	On the RAP application, the borrower is informed through their consent that their data can be exchanged with the CRA.

b.	If the borrower has indicated that they are in a married or common-law relationship, then consent, name, date of birth, SIN, and income of their partner will be provided to the SP. A signed consent of the borrower and their spouse must be received prior to any data exchange between the SP and CRA. Spousal signature is a prerequisite for the adjudication of the RAP application.

c.	If the application is complete, SP calculates whether a borrower is eligible for RAP. 

2.	Selection: Those borrowers that have been identified as high risk for misrepresentation based on the risk-based sampling methods may be selected for verification using the RAP-EVM. Borrowers that are not selected for verification are assessed through the regular RAP process, including calculation (#9). RAP calculation precedes selection.

3.	Request for Information: The SP securely sends an electronic request for information to the CRA, including the name, SIN, and date of birth of the borrower and the borrower’s married/common-law partner (if applicable), to confirm previous year’s income (Line 15000 of the borrower’s tax return), marital status, and the number of dependants in the family.

4.	Response to Request: The CRA provides the aforementioned borrower's tax data to the SP.

a.	The response would occur via a secure electronic file transmission.

b.	The response would include the names, date of birth, and SIN of borrowers (and their married/common-law partner, if applicable) as well as their Line 15000 income, number of dependants and marital status. The CRA will also confirm the tax year of the data (the most recent data from the last two years requested only). If the response returns with no data, adjudication process moves to #7.

5.	Data Comparison: The SP compares Line 15000 of the borrower’s income data , as provided by the CRA to the borrower’s reported income on their RAP application. 

a.	Disparities beyond a “threshold of tolerance” in income between a borrower’s CRA tax data and the income declared on the RAP application would justify a request for additional proof from the borrower. The same would apply for inconsistencies with declarations of ‘married’ or ‘single’ and instances where borrowers report a higher number of dependants on their RAP application. The “threshold of tolerance” is determined by the CSFA Program.

b.	The SP annualizes RAP application income data before comparison with CRA borrower (and spouse/common-law partner) income data.

c.	If a family size discrepancy is identified, but the verified family income is less than the family income threshold, additional proof would not be requested since it would not make a difference for adjudication.

6.	Assessment Part One: If the borrower’s declared CRA income, family size and married/common-law status are consistent with the information reported on the RAP application, or if business rules deem no further validation required, the borrower will be assessed for RAP under the existing process.

7.	Assessment Part Two: If there is a variance between their RAP application data and their CRA tax data, or if the CRA is unable to provide the borrower's tax data, the borrower is asked to provide additional substantiating documentation.
8.	Assessment Part Three: Based on the borrower’s declared income and any supporting documentation, the SP proceeds to finalize the assessment of the borrower’s RAP application.

9.	Final Calculation and Decision: The SP runs a final calculation of eligibility, based on verified family income and size, unchanged or revised based on the requested borrower documentation. If the borrower provides additional proof documentation that differs from their RAP application data, their RAP benefit is re-calculated based on the new information.

10.	End of Process: The borrower is informed of the status of their application.
